PROSPECTING AND QUALIFYING
The Straight Line isn’t about closing everybody. What this is about is making sure you’re talking
to the right people and then getting those people who are on the fence—the ones reserved only for
the very best salespeople up until now—to say ‘yes.’
Introduction to Prospecting & Qualifying
Prospecting & Qualifying Introduction
Prospecting & Qualifying Introduction
The Straight Line isn’t about closing everybody. There are some people who simply aren’t
closable, and then there are others who simply shouldn’t be closed. What this is about is making
sure you’re talking to the right people and then getting those people who are on the fence—the
ones reserved only for the very best salespeople up until now—to say ‘yes.’
It is NOT about closing EVERY SINGLE PERSON you contact!
Through qualifying, you should build a huge amount of rapport and find out as much information
as you need to later close the sale. If you focus just on the money and whether they can afford what
you’re selling, you have fallen well short of what you can achieve through qualifying.
In qualifying prospects you are not only entitled, but OBLIGATED to ask questions in order to
discover what the person’s needs are and where their pain is. If you don’t really care and are only
thinking about closing, this will be obvious to the prospect and they will block you. But if you
qualify elegantly, you will be perceived as a trusted advisor who truly understands and cares.
Closing will follow naturally.

-- 1 of 11 --

Access the rest of this module to learn about:
 How to identify ‘nuggets of gold’ – sifting for your gold prospects.
 The sales funnel – getting clear on how to reach your financial goals.
 Buying archetypes – knowing what they look like in your industry.
 The three keys to qualifying – identifying prospect needs, gathering information and
determining pain points.
 Qualifying effectively – structuring your questions, using appropriate tonality, listening
effectively, and how to elegantly transition from qualifying into the body of your
presentation.
 How to identify and focus on the people that matter – getting the best sales results in the
shortest period of time by focusing prospects who want or need your product or service.
Nuggets of Gold
Nuggets of Gold

-- 2 of 11 --

Imagine a prospector in the 1930’s, mining for gold in a mountain stream. They typically sat
streamside with a pan, sifting through countless gallons of water, looking for one nugget of gold.
What they didn’t do is sift through gallons of water, expecting to turn the water into gold. Just like
these gold prospectors, you don’t want to try to turn water into gold. Prospecting is not about
alchemy. It’s about influence and vision!
There are two things you must consistently do in order to successfully qualify your prospects and
determine who the individuals are that you want to actually make presentations to.
You must:
 Be quick, elegant, and non-alienating
 Use a script
When you are prospecting, the biggest mistake you can make is to try and use your influence,
tonality, and other tools to turn non-buyers into buyers. Instead, go through your sales funnel and
sift out the people who are potential buyers; the nuggets of gold.
The Sales Funnel
The Sales Funnel
The Sales Funnel

-- 3 of 11 --

The only way to effectively reach your goal is to know precisely what your goal is and what it will
take to get there. The Sales Funnel is a visual concept that will assist you in doing this by breaking
down your various activities into chunks to get the results you are after. It’s important to
understand the actual numbers needed to reach your own personal goal and work backwards from
there.
The sales funnel example shows that you might dial the phone with 120 outgoing calls in a week.
From those calls, you might only connect with 40 people. From those 40 people, you probably get
eight appointments to see them in person or simply to close them on the phone or call them back—
the two call system – or in person. Of those eight appointments, a couple will cancel or not show
up.
So you get six actual interviews where you can sit down and try to influence the person and close
him or her. Of those, you might close a third. You’ll get two sales in a week. If you were getting
$2,000 net commission, this equates to $4,000 that you’d make per week or approximately
$200,000 per year.
The point is that this is all structured, but the way you actually do this is work backwards.

-- 4 of 11 --

Four Buying Archetypes
Four Buying Archetypes
Buying Archetypes
Now that you know what your numbers are, you are ready to come into contact with potential
buyers. It’s important to realize that prospects can typically be classified into four different types.
These ‘buying archetypes’ are the four different classes of people you will come into contact with
when you go out and start prospecting. The four different archetypes are:
• Archetype 1: ‘Ready’
• Archetype 2: ‘Still shopping around’
• Archetype 3: ‘Curious’
• Archetype 4: ‘Dragged and won’t do it’

-- 5 of 11 --

In order to quickly identify which Archetype you are dealing with you need to think about what
each Archetype profile would look like, specific to your own product, industry and/or service.
Keys To Qualifying
3 Keys To Qualifying
One of the biggest mistakes that salespeople make when they are identifying needs is that they use
their own preconceived notions of what a prospect might want or what they are thinking.
Key 1: Identifying Needs
Key 1: Identifying Needs
Start developing your qualifying questions. Keep in mind any industry-specific considerations and
your long term outcome at all times

-- 6 of 11 --

Key 2: Intelligence Gathering
Key 2: Maintain Rapport While Qualifying
When you are asking qualifying questions of prospects, you are far less likely to encounter
resistance if you give a reason – a JUSTIFIER.
Some useful justifiers in sales situations include: “Let me ask you a couple of quick questions so
I don’t waste your time”
OR
“Do you mind if I ask you a couple of questions so I can find out exactly what you need?”
Research shows that it doesn’t really matter what the justification is people will be more likely to
comply – just as long as you have one.
So always give a reason for asking a qualifying question.
Key 3: Amplifying Pain
Key 3: Amplify Their Pain

-- 7 of 11 --

Ensure that your prospect is feeling the pain or the negative impact of their situation. Do this by
asking questions in an offhand manner and also by using a tonality that conveys that you care and
are sensitive to their situation.
Syntax For Qualifying
The Order of Qualifying
REMEMBER, A USEFUL SYNTAX FOR ASKING QUALIFYING QUESTIONS IS:
• Ask questions, using justifiers
• Get answers and make mental notes
• Ask more questions in response to their answers, exhausting a logical line of questioning
• Discover and amplify their pain in an ethical and sensitive way if necessary
• Move onto the next line of questioning
AND DON’T TRY AND SOLVE THEIR PAIN PREMATURELY!

-- 8 of 11 --

Qualifying Effectively
Qualifying Effectively
Know Your Questions
Structuring Your Qualifying Questions
Make sure you structure your qualifying questions from big picture to more detailed questions.

-- 9 of 11 --

Use The Right Tonality
Using Tonality
Every question has its own tonality. Practice this in advance. For example, when you ask, “How
long have you been thinking about trading?” you must sound very interested, so that they know
you care. When they know you care, you chip away at a core belief and the client will match your
caring tone.
For more sensitive or invasive questions, an apologetic tone is often appropriate. Conveying that
you empathise with them will help them feel comfortable opening up.
Consider your 3 ‘Big Picture’ questions from ‘structuring your qualifying questions’. Write down
the tonality you are going to use when delivering each of them. Remember to practice your
qualifying questions and their related tonalities until they become second nature.
It’s critically important to use the right tonality when you are asking more invasive questions. For
example, asking the client how long they have been thinking about refinancing their home, trading
some stock or whatever it is you are trying to sell. This question could potentially elicit a cache of
information, so listen carefully.
Some examples are:
“What’s your annual salary, just a ball park?”
“How long have you been thinking about buying a Porsche?”
“How long have you been thinking about this piece of equipment?”
It is important to convey the message that you care about your client and his/her specific situation.
Keep this in mind throughout your questioning.
Other invasive questions are:

-- 10 of 11 --

“How much money do you have in the stock market?”
“How much do you owe on your mortgage right now?”
Tips For Qualifying Effectively
The Transition
The Transition To The Main Presentation
After you are done asking questions it’s time for the transition into setting an appointment or
whatever outcome you are looking for. A good transition phrase is, “Well, based on everything
you just told me, it sounds like this is a perfect fit for you.”
Use this statement exactly like this, as it will become an anchor for you. Then follow it with the
main body of your presentation.

-- 11 of 11 --
